content.text: Self-service API key and quota provisioning is a platform capability that allows developers to request, configure, and receive LLM API keys and spending quotas through an automated portal or CLI without involving a platform team member in the approval loop. Instead of submitting a ticket and waiting for a human to provision credentials and set limits, a developer authenticates to the internal developer portal, selects their service and environment, specifies the quota tier they need, and the system automatically provisions a scoped API key, stores it in the team's vault path, and configures the associated spending limit — all in under a minute.

The 'self-service' aspect does not mean uncontrolled: policy rules embedded in the provisioning service determine which quota tiers a team can access based on their environment (sandbox vs. production), their service registration in the catalog, and their cost center attribution. High quota tiers may require cost center specification and manager approval for the first request but are then available self-service for subsequent requests. The system logs every provisioning action with full attribution, enabling finance, security, and compliance teams to audit key usage and cost attribution without involving the platform team.

API key provisioning bottlenecks slow down development more than almost any other infrastructure dependency. A developer who cannot start testing their agent without waiting three days for a ticket to be resolved will either find a workaround (sharing credentials from another service, using a personal API key) or lose momentum. Both outcomes are worse than the alternative: shared credentials are a security risk, and lost developer momentum compounds across a team of ten engineers into significant lost throughput.

Self-service provisioning also improves security posture compared to manual processes. Manual provisioning often results in keys being shared informally, keys with excessive scopes because requesting specific scopes requires re-submitting a ticket, and keys that are never rotated because rotation requires another ticket. Automated provisioning creates keys with the minimum necessary scope for the service and environment, stores them in the vault rather than in Slack messages or email threads, and enables automatic rotation on a defined schedule.
Developers receive provisioned credentials in under a minute rather than days, eliminating a common development bottleneck.
Keys are scoped to the requesting service and environment, reducing the blast radius of a compromised key.
Vault storage is the default path, preventing credentials from living in chat messages or configuration files.
Cost center attribution at provisioning time enables accurate cost allocation without manual reconciliation.
Every provisioning action is logged with requestor, timestamp, quota tier, and justification, enabling compliance audit.
Automated key rotation on a schedule can be initiated by the provisioning service without developer action.
Self-service increases the risk of over-provisioning if quota tiers are not well-calibrated or if teams request the highest tier by default.
Automated provisioning requires a robust identity system to verify that requestors are who they claim to be.
The provisioning service becomes a high-availability dependency: if it goes down, new keys cannot be provisioned during the outage.
Managing quota enforcement across multiple LLM providers requires provider-specific integration work.
Emergency key revocation must work even when the standard provisioning flow is unavailable.

The provisioning service exposes an API and a web UI. Developers authenticate using their corporate SSO credentials; the provisioning service verifies their identity and looks up their team membership and service registrations in the internal catalog. When a developer requests a key for a specific service and environment, the provisioning service evaluates the request against policy rules: is the service registered? Is the requested quota tier appropriate for the environment? Does the cost center code exist? Policy evaluation happens synchronously; most requests are approved without human intervention.

Once approved, the provisioning service generates a scoped API key through the LLM provider's API (Anthropic, OpenAI, or others), stores the key in the team's Vault path with appropriate ACLs, and records the provisioning event in the audit log. The quota configuration is stored in the rate limiting and budget enforcement service, which intercepts all outbound LLM API calls from the team's services and enforces the daily token limit and monthly spending cap. When usage approaches 80% of the budget, the provisioning service sends an alert to the team's notification channel; at 100%, calls begin failing with a budget-exceeded error.

Key rotation is handled through the same provisioning service. Teams can trigger manual rotation from the portal or configure automatic rotation on a schedule (monthly is common). The rotation process generates a new key, stores it in the vault at the same path, and invalidates the old key — all in sequence so that services reading the key from the vault automatically pick up the new one on their next vault read without downtime.

attrs.alt: Self-service API key and quota provisioning architecture

content.text: Implement self-service provisioning when the organization reaches five or more teams actively using LLM APIs, at which point the volume of provisioning requests exceeds what a platform team can handle as a manual workflow without creating a bottleneck. Earlier, a simple ticket-based process is sufficient. Later, the accumulated security debt from workaround credential sharing will be significant.

Prioritize the audit logging and quota enforcement components alongside the self-service provisioning flow, not as a later addition. A self-service system without audit logs is harder to secure than a manual system — you lose the human reviewer as a control point without gaining an automated record. Audit logs are necessary to detect abuse, attribute costs, and satisfy security audit requirements.

# provisioning_service/api.py — provisioning endpoint (FastAPI)
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal
import anthropic, hvac, boto3, uuid, time

app = FastAPI()

class ProvisionRequest(BaseModel):
    service_name: str
    environment: Literal["sandbox", "staging", "production"]
    quota_tier: Literal["sandbox", "standard", "production", "enterprise"]
    cost_center: str
    justification: str

class ProvisionResponse(BaseModel):
    vault_path: str
    quota_daily_tokens: int
    quota_monthly_usd: float
    key_id: str  # reference ID for rotation/revocation

QUOTA_TIERS = {
    "sandbox":    {"tokens_per_day": 50_000,    "monthly_usd": 5.0},
    "standard":   {"tokens_per_day": 500_000,   "monthly_usd": 50.0},
    "production": {"tokens_per_day": 5_000_000, "monthly_usd": 500.0},
    "enterprise": {"tokens_per_day": -1,         "monthly_usd": -1},  # custom
}

def get_current_user(request) -> dict:
    # Validate JWT, return {user: "alice", team: "platform-sre"}
    ...

@app.post("/provision")
async def provision_key(req: ProvisionRequest, user=Depends(get_current_user)):
    # 1. Policy evaluation
    if req.environment == "production" and req.quota_tier == "enterprise":
        # Enterprise tier requires manager approval (async workflow, not shown)
        raise HTTPException(status_code=202, detail="Approval pending")

    # 2. Generate scoped API key via provider API
    anthropic_client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=PLATFORM_MASTER_KEY)
    key = anthropic_client.api_keys.create(
        name=f"{user['team']}-{req.service_name}-{req.environment}",
        # Scope to minimum necessary permissions
    )

    # 3. Store in Vault
    vault = hvac.Client(url=VAULT_ADDR, token=VAULT_TOKEN)
    vault_path = f"secret/teams/{user['team']}/{req.service_name}/{req.environment}/llm-api-key"
    vault.secrets.kv.v2.create_or_update_secret(
        path=vault_path, secret={"value": key.secret_key}
    )

    # 4. Configure quota
    quota = QUOTA_TIERS[req.quota_tier]
    configure_quota(req.service_name, req.environment, quota)

    # 5. Audit log
    audit_log(user=user["user"], service=req.service_name, env=req.environment,
              tier=req.quota_tier, cost_center=req.cost_center, key_id=key.id)

    return ProvisionResponse(
        vault_path=vault_path,
        quota_daily_tokens=quota["tokens_per_day"],
        quota_monthly_usd=quota["monthly_usd"],
        key_id=key.id,
    )

# CLI usage — self-service provisioning from terminal
# Install the internal platform CLI
pip install platform-cli --index-url https://pypi.internal.company.com

# Provision a key for your service
platform keys provision \
  --service alert-router \
  --environment staging \
  --tier standard \
  --cost-center CC-1042 \
  --justification "New agent service for SRE alert routing"

# Output:
# Key provisioned successfully.
# Vault path: secret/teams/platform-sre/alert-router/staging/llm-api-key
# Quota: 500,000 tokens/day, $50/month cap
# Key ID: key_01abc123 (use this for rotation/revocation)

# Rotate an existing key
platform keys rotate --key-id key_01abc123

# Check current usage
platform keys usage --service alert-router --environment staging

A developer on the data-tools team creates a new agent service for document summarization. She visits the internal developer portal, authenticates via SSO, and fills out the provisioning form: service name `doc-summarizer`, environment `sandbox`, quota tier `sandbox` (50k tokens/day), cost center CC-2087. The provisioning service validates her team membership, confirms the cost center exists, generates a key, stores it in `secret/teams/data-tools/doc-summarizer/sandbox/llm-api-key`, and returns the vault path. She updates her service's Kubernetes configuration to reference the vault path, and is writing tests within two minutes of opening the portal.

Three months later, the doc-summarizer service is ready for production. The developer provisions a production-tier key through the same portal flow: the provisioning service notes that the production tier for her team requires a cost center confirmation (already provided) and checks that the service is registered in the catalog as a production service (it is, per the pre-production checklist). The key is provisioned automatically with a 5M token/day limit and $500/month cap. The team's Slack channel receives a notification confirming the provisioning and the quota limits.

When the monthly cost alert fires at 80% of the $500 cap after three weeks of production traffic, the developer uses the portal to review her service's token consumption breakdown by model and endpoint. The breakdown reveals that one summarization endpoint is making redundant LLM calls due to a missing cache layer. She fixes the caching bug and requests a quota tier review after two months of corrected usage data to confirm that the standard tier would be sufficient for their actual workload.

Anthropic's own developer console provides self-service API key management for external customers: developers can create multiple keys scoped to specific use cases, set usage limits per key, view per-key consumption, and rotate or revoke keys without contacting support. Internal platforms implementing self-service provisioning for teams using Anthropic or OpenAI APIs follow the same pattern but add corporate identity integration (SSO rather than email/password), cost center attribution, vault storage rather than copy-paste delivery, and budget enforcement that fires alerts through the team's notification channels rather than billing the developer's personal credit card. The core user experience model — immediate self-service, scoped keys, visible consumption, easy rotation — is proven at Anthropic's scale of millions of API keys.
Provisioning keys to developers directly (to their local .env files) rather than to vault paths, creating an audit and rotation problem.
Not scoping keys to the requesting service and environment, so a compromised key for a sandbox service can access production quotas.
Setting quota limits too high by default to avoid alert fatigue, losing the cost control benefit of quota enforcement.
Not logging provisioning events with full attribution, making cost reconciliation and security audit impossible.
Building key rotation as a separate workflow from provisioning, so teams never rotate because it requires a different tool.
Failing to handle vault unavailability gracefully in services: a service that crashes when it cannot read its key from vault at startup is fragile.
Make vault storage the only delivery mechanism for provisioned keys; never display raw keys in the portal UI after creation.
Pre-populate cost center codes and service names from the internal catalog to reduce input errors and enable attribution.
Configure budget alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100% of the monthly cap, not just at 100% — teams need warning before service disruption.
Build key rotation into the same portal flow as provisioning; rotation should be a one-click action, not a multi-step process.
Log provisioning events to the same audit log pipeline used for security events, not to a separate system that may not be monitored.
Test key revocation paths regularly in a non-production environment; revocation must work under incident conditions when the standard provisioning flow may be degraded.

Key provisioning is a low-frequency operation — a team may provision or rotate a key once a month — so throughput of the provisioning service is rarely a bottleneck. Availability is more important than throughput: if the provisioning service is down when a team needs to rotate a compromised key urgently, the incident cannot be contained quickly. Deploy the provisioning service with the same SLO as other critical platform infrastructure and ensure that key revocation can be performed through an emergency path that does not depend on the full provisioning service being available.

Quota enforcement, by contrast, is a high-throughput operation executed on every LLM API call. The quota enforcement layer must add minimal latency to the request path — under 1ms in the synchronous path. Implement enforcement using an in-process or sidecar rate limiter that reads quota configuration from a fast local cache (Redis or in-memory) rather than querying the provisioning service on every request. The provisioning service updates the rate limiter configuration when quotas are changed; the rate limiter handles enforcement.
Redis + sliding window, Envoy rate limit
Must be sub-millisecond in hot path
Provider spend APIs vary in granularity

The provisioning service has access to master LLM API credentials to generate child keys — this makes it a high-value attack target. Run the provisioning service in an isolated network segment with no public internet exposure. Use a dedicated vault AppRole with narrow permissions (only the ability to write to team paths) rather than a vault root token. Rotate the provisioning service's own credentials on a schedule and monitor for anomalous provisioning activity (an unusual number of keys provisioned from a single identity in a short window is a signal worth alerting on).

Reliability of the vault storage path is critical for service startup: if a service cannot read its API key from vault at startup, it cannot initialize. Implement a cache that persists the most recently read key to the service's local filesystem (encrypted) so that short vault outages do not cause service restarts. The cached key should expire after a shorter TTL than the vault lease duration, ensuring that rotated keys are picked up promptly while providing resilience against temporary vault unavailability.
Self-service provisioning eliminates a common development bottleneck and improves security by making the correct, secure workflow the easiest one.
Keys must be stored in vault at provisioning time; delivering keys through the portal UI bypasses the secure storage requirement.
Quota tiers with policy-based auto-approval allow most provisioning requests to complete in seconds without human review.
Budget alerts at 80% and 100% of monthly caps prevent surprise cost overruns and enable teams to optimize consumption proactively.
Every provisioning and revocation event must be logged with full attribution for security audit and cost reconciliation.
Emergency key revocation must be available through a path that does not depend on the full provisioning service being healthy.
